meta.title: Miguel in the Secret Garden
meta.author: Lori Polydoros
meta.focusQuestion: How does Miguel's magical adventure as a robin transform his understanding of gardens and his father's wisdom?

sequenceEvents.summary: Miguel groans about working in the family garden and takes a break at the loft, where he finds The Secret Garden book.
sequenceEvents.detail: Miguel Ventura was exhausted from weeding in the family garden. He wanted adventure and danger, not peace. At the loft, he found a colorful copy of The Secret Garden atop the chest of Great Gallardo's magic books.

sequenceEvents.summary: The chest locks and Miguel is transported into the story as the robin from The Secret Garden.
sequenceEvents.detail: Miguel tried to choose a different book, but the chest locked. The words on page 181 scrambled. Dizziness swept over him like a wave, and a gust of wind lifted him high above. He discovered he had wings and feathers instead of arms—he was the robin!

sequenceEvents.summary: Miguel discovers the secret garden entrance but cannot communicate with Mary because his words come out as bird chatter.
sequenceEvents.detail: Flying above the grounds, Miguel spotted a hidden doorknob in the ivy-covered wall. But when he tried to tell Mary where to go, his words came out as chirps and cheeps. His robin talk was indecipherable to everyone.

sequenceEvents.summary: Miguel meets Dickon, who can talk to animals, but the fox attacks Miguel and breaks his wing.
sequenceEvents.detail: Miguel hoped Dickon could understand him, but his bird chatter was indecipherable. The fox's eyes narrowed and it lunged. Miguel tried to fly away but was swatted down. A pain shot through his body, and all went dark.

sequenceEvents.summary: With a broken wing, Miguel leads Mary and Dickon through the manor to Colin's room, then guides them to the secret garden.
sequenceEvents.detail: Miguel hopped out of Dickon's pocket and urged them to follow. They walked down endless dark mahogany hallways to Colin's room. The next day, despite his broken wing, Miguel led them to the secret garden, and Mary turned the key in the lock.

sequenceEvents.summary: Miguel returns home transformed, gives his dad a lavender rose, and begins working happily in the garden.
sequenceEvents.detail: Back in the loft, Miguel found a lavender rose on the chest. He handed it to his dad and said, 'Thanks. For this.' He grabbed a shovel and began working alongside his father, finally understanding that gardens are places of healing.

sections.title: Groaning About Gardening
paragraphs: Roses. Oregano. Raspberries. Miguel Ventura was exhausted. The weeds. The thorns. The smells. Working in the family garden was not how he planned to spend his summer.
“We need your help,” his dad said, “to grow food for the shop.” Miguel and his little sister, Teresa, often lent a hand at the Ventura family sandwich shop.
“Gardens,” Miguel’s dad glanced up, “can be magic.”
“Yeah, right.”
“The Earth has healing powers.” His dad cut a bouquet of yellow roses. “The peace would do you some good.”
“I don’t want peace,” Miguel said. “I want adventure and danger!”
Dad rolled his eyes.

Miguel took a break in the coolness of the shop. Mom and Teresa were baking bread, so he sneaked away to the loft. He was in desperate need of The Great Gallardo’s books. A while back, Miguel had discovered a chest full of magic books that belonged to his great-grandfather George, a magician called The Great Gallardo. Miguel went into these stories as an actual character. He’d become Huckleberry Finn from The Adventures of Tom Sawyer and the Scarecrow from The Wizard of Oz. Every time Miguel entered a story, he usually had to solve a big problem. Sometimes it was pretty dangerous. And danger was what Miguel needed.

Up at the loft, a red feather rested near the rusty lock of the chest. When Miguel first chose one of The Great Gallardo’s books, he usually found a strange object from the story. Miguel spent hours trying to figure out how he became a real, live character in a book. Was it a spell? An enchantment? The Great Gallardo’s magic could be risky. Maybe it was a curse.

A colorful book sat atop all the others. He picked it up. The Secret Garden. It was a good book he remembered from the beginning of the year, but it wasn’t dangerous enough. Miguel tried to choose another. But the lid had closed. The chest was locked!

sections.title: Bird Talk
paragraphs: Once a book had magically been chosen for him, there was no going back, so he opened to page 181. “The robin flew from his swinging spray of ivy . . . ” The words scrambled on the page. “. . . trill on of the and he loud, opened top his an sang beak a wall lovely.”
Dizziness swept over Miguel like a wave. He closed his eyes, and a gust of wind swept him up, lifting him high above until he felt weightless—like air. Was he flying?
Miguel was flying! He soared miles and miles above green pasture. With each gust of wind, the air flowed under him, lifting him higher. He rocketed through the air, flapping hard . . . FLAPPING?
Miguel tried to extend his arms but found that he had wings and feathers instead. “I’m the robin!” he said as he began to flap his wings.

Never in any Great Gallardo book had Miguel become an animal. He pointed his beak and dove downward. The ground rushed up at him as though he were fast-forwarding a movie on a screen. With wings spread wide, the wind brushed against him, but the feathers insulated him from the cold. Being a bird was incredible!
A big gust of wind rushed through the oak trees and lifted trailing sprays of untrimmed ivy. During the gust, Miguel noticed a wall between the vines. Could this be the secret garden in the book?

Swooping down with the next gust of wind, he saw things in patches, almost as though he were looking at a map, one grid at a time. Details popped out as though they were three-dimensional. Miguel zeroed in on a section of the brick wall. He fluttered around and around the ivy, until one more big rush of wind lifted up a thick patch, revealing a round doorknob. It was the entrance to the secret garden!

A small wave of excitement flittered through his feathers, but his first instinct about this story soon returned. This was not going to be the exciting adventure he hoped for. He regretted having climbed into the loft.
A whistling sound drew him down and around the garden, where he found a man tending the roses. “That has to be Ben, one of the groundskeepers!”
Miguel fluttered above his head.
“You’re at it again, today, robin,” Ben said. “Mary is over there!”

Miguel rose on an updraft and cruised above the trees again. He caught sight of a young girl searching in the orchard as though she’d lost something. Miguel let a current of air gently lower him to a branch of ivy. “Follow me, Mary!” he squawked at her. “There’s something I must show you.”
Mary stared at him and cocked her head.
He quickly remembered that robins didn’t speak English; his words were only chirps and cheeps. He tried again, but no matter what he said, it came out something like cheerily, cheer up, cheer up.
How could he get Mary to understand him? He hopped up and down, flapped his wings, and bobbed his head.
“What a strange bird,” Mary said.

Miguel shook his head back and forth. He stood on one foot. To get her attention, Miguel belted out the best bird-song he could muster. But as hard as he tried, the song came out sounding like the whispery words hisselly-hisselly.
Mary smiled. “Thank you for the song, dear robin, but I’m trying to find a lost garden that was my aunt’s favorite place.” She skipped away down the path, leaving a most sorrowful bird behind.
This was going to be tougher than he thought. Miguel’s frustration was building. How could he talk to her if she didn’t speak his language?

sections.title: A Snack for a Fox
paragraphs: After a little nap in the oaks, Miguel awoke to the grumblings of his stomach. He flittered down onto a rock. A tiny school of fish headed downstream in a creek. Too fishy for lunch. A squiggly earthworm squirmed in the mud below him. Too mushy for a snack. Miguel sighed. If he didn’t find something to eat soon, he’d have no energy to help Mary find the secret garden.

He flew up and away when he noticed a large patch of land lined with rows and rows of vining plants. As he landed in the field, a sweet scent wafted through the air. It was a vineyard full of plump purple grapes—just right for lunch! He pecked and pecked away at the fruit, which restored a bit of his body’s energy with each morsel he ate.

Amid the feast, Miguel heard a strange sound. It was soft like a flute, but lighter, almost like trees blowing in the breeze. A boy with rosy cheeks walked up to him, flute in hand. “Hello, fair robin.” Several squirrels and a small rabbit peeked out of pockets on his worn jacket. A little red fox followed at his heels. This must be Mary’s friend Dickon; he had a knack for talking with animals. Surely he could help Miguel.
“I need to find Mary,” Miguel chirped.
“Slow down—I don’t speak robin very well,” Dickon chuckled as he sat down on a stump. The fox hopped up to his lap.

“I need to find a girl named Mary.” Miguel tried to speak clearly, but he knew that each word was merely bird chatter. “Mary. Mary. MARY!” His words spewed out like a bird alarm, tuktuk, tuktuk, tuktuk!

Robin talk was indecipherable! Miguel stared at Dickon, willing him to understand his request. But instead, the dark eyes of the fox stared back. Miguel froze, quickly realizing that he was the perfect size for a tasty treat.
“Tell me again, robin,” Dickon said.
But before Miguel could utter one little chirp, the fox’s eyes narrowed and its ears stood erect.
Miguel’s heart raced. He had to get out of there—NOW! With a swivel of his head, Miguel lifted his wings.
The fox lunged.
“Stop!” cried Dickon.
Miguel glanced back. He flapped his wings but found it difficult to fly in a panic.
He ran forward, but every time he tried to lift off, the fox sped closer.
For a moment, his wings seemed useless, so he hid in a hollow log. A narrow snout shoved its way in. Miguel screeched. Fluttering out the other side, he waddled forward, flapping his wings.
His feet floated off the ground. He flapped. Pump. Pump. Pump.
Just as he began to fly, the fox swatted him down.
A pain shot through his body. He hit the ground with a thud.
He tried to stand, but his wings felt heavy, as though he wore an iron blanket. The eyes of the fox burned into his. Miguel’s eyes fluttered. Then all went dark.

sections.title: Broken Wing
paragraphs: Miguel awoke to warmth—a cozy feeling that reminded him of being tucked away in his bed at home on a rainy night. He blinked his robin eyes, and soon things became clear. Miguel sat inside a box filled with shredded cloth. He was still alive!
“Don’t fret, dear robin.” Dickon stroked his wings. “You’re safe.”
Dickon’s house had a fire burning in the hearth, and herbs and dried fruit hung from hooks along the wall. And best of all, there was no fox!
“I do apologize for the impoliteness of my fox,” Dickon said. “Sometimes, nature gets the best of him.”
Miguel’s right wing ached, a constant jabbing pain as though he’d broken his arm. How would he ever take Mary to the garden door if he couldn’t even fly?
“I must get Mary to the garden!” Miguel chirped, lifting one wing.
“It’s broken,” Dickon said. “But I will care for you.”
Could Dickon understand him? “I must get to the garden!” he chirped.
Before Dickon could respond, a knock came from the small wooden door. Miguel took in a deep breath as it creaked open.
Mary!
Miguel chirped and hollered and sang a chorus of robin song. He hopped up as best he could with a broken wing and bobbed his head.
“I think he has a message for you!” Dickon ushered Mary over to the robin.
“That strange bird again,” she said. “He behaved that way yesterday.”

“The door to the garden!” Miguel chattered. “I know where it is!” With all the strength he could find, he hopped out of the box and onto the table. Ever so carefully, Miguel jumped down to a chair, then onto the floor. Pain flashed through his wing. He waddled over to the door, standing there, staring forward.
“I think he wants to show us something,” Mary said.
“But he can’t fly.” Dickon scooped him up.
“No, but I’ve got the perfect idea,” Miguel said. “Follow me!”

Tucked away in Dickon’s pocket, Miguel led them out of the small house and up the path toward the manor. Like a game of hot and cold, he chirped and whistled to keep them moving in the right direction. Inside the house, they walked down endless dark mahogany hallways lined with doors. Finally, Miguel took them to the final door. This was the room of Colin—the boy who most needed the secret garden’s healing powers.

Miguel hopped out of Dickon’s pocket and urged Dickon to open the door. From the bed, Colin stirred and sat up. Miguel climbed up into Colin’s wheelchair and sang a celebratory song. Miguel knew the three would quickly become friends.

sections.title: Growing Magic
paragraphs: The next day, with Colin in his wheelchair and the robin nestled in his lap, Miguel used his song to lead his friends through the pasture and down the pathway to the oaks and ivy surrounding the secret garden.
Despite his broken wing, Miguel managed to flitter onto the piece of ivy near the doorknob.
“The door to the secret garden!” Mary pulled out a key, stuck it in the lock, and turned it. Click.
As they walked in, Miguel felt a tingle of energy surge through his body.
Colin gasped. Mary ran in circles. Dickon laughed with joy. They had found the long sought-after garden!

However, the once-beautiful garden now had vines covering everything, and most of the other plants seemed dry and lifeless. But Miguel knew the trio could bring it back to life. Miguel knew that because of his help, the poppies, roses, and hollyhock would soon be in full bloom. Mary would be entertaining Colin and Dickon, and Colin would need his wheelchair less and less. Maybe Miguel’s dad was right about gardens. They are places of healing.

Miguel lifted off the ground, encircling the garden a few times. He looked down on the three smiling kids and then shot through the clouds. Before he knew it, he was home in the quiet loft. A lavender rose lay on top of the chest.
Back outside, Miguel’s dad was still pruning the roses. He held a bouquet full of colors, all except lavender. For the first time, Miguel found he enjoyed the sweet scent of roses.
His dad looked up. “Back from your break?”
Miguel nodded. He grabbed a shovel, slipped on his gloves, and put on a cap.
“This is for you,” he said, handing his dad the lavender rose. “Thanks.”
“For what?”
“For this.” He waved his arms around the garden and then started working.

vocabulary.definition: urgent or extreme; showing great need or desire
vocabulary.sentence: He was in desperate need of The Great Gallardo’s books.

vocabulary.definition: impulse or reaction; a natural behavior that is not learned
vocabulary.sentence: A small wave of excitement flittered through his feathers, but his first instinct about this story soon returned.

vocabulary.definition: to summon or bring forth; to gather up
vocabulary.sentence: To get her attention, Miguel belted out the best bird-song he could muster.
His words spewed out like a bird alarm, tuktuk, tuktuk, tuktuk!

vocabulary.definition: to turn, as in a circle; to rotate around a central point
vocabulary.sentence: With a swivel of his head, Miguel lifted his wings.

vocabulary.definition: used to celebrate, or show happiness about a positive event
vocabulary.sentence: Miguel climbed up into Colin’s wheelchair and sang a celebratory song.

options: used to celebrate or show happiness about a positive event

vocabulary.definition: likely to cause harm or injury; risky
vocabulary.sentence: Sometimes it was pretty dangerous. And danger was what Miguel needed.

options: likely to cause harm or injury

vocabulary.sentence: With wings spread wide, the wind brushed against him, but the feathers insulated him from the cold.

options: left you exposed to the elements
protected you from heat or cold

vocabulary.definition: a type of wood with a rich reddish-brown color
vocabulary.sentence: Inside the house, they walked down endless dark mahogany hallways lined with doors.

options: a type of glass used in windows
a metal used to build tools
a type of wood with a rich reddish-brown color
a fabric used to make clothing

vocabulary.definition: having height, width, and depth; appearing to have depth
vocabulary.sentence: Details popped out as though they were three-dimensional.

vocabulary.definition: an air current that moves upward
vocabulary.sentence: Miguel rose on an updraft and cruised above the trees again.

options: an air current that moves upward

questions.question: How did Miguel’s attitude about working in the garden change from the beginning to the end of this story?
questions.evidence: At the beginning: “I don’t want peace,” Miguel said. “I want adventure and danger!” At the end: He grabbed a shovel, slipped on his gloves, and put on a cap. “This is for you,” he said, handing his dad the lavender rose. “Thanks.”
choices: At first he was unmotivated to work, but at the end he was eager to help.
At first he was excited about it, but at the end he was discouraged.
When the story opened, he was happy about the garden, but at the end he was confused.
He first felt like no one appreciated him, but in the end he learned his family understood his talent.
questions.explanation: At the beginning, Miguel groaned about working in the garden and wanted adventure instead. By the end, after his magical adventure as a robin, he returned and willingly grabbed a shovel to help his dad. He even thanked his father with a lavender rose, showing he now appreciated the garden.

questions.question: When something swivels, it ___.
questions.evidence: With a swivel of his head, Miguel lifted his wings.
choices: rises up
turns in a circle
gets covered up
becomes tangled
questions.explanation: The glossary defines 'swivel' as 'turn, as in a circle.' In the story, Miguel turns (swivels) his head quickly when he needs to escape from the fox.

questions.question: What happened to Miguel after he picked up the book, The Secret Garden?
questions.evidence: Miguel tried to choose another. But the lid had closed. The chest was locked! Once a book had magically been chosen for him, there was no going back, so he opened to page 181. The words scrambled on the page.
choices: The lid to the chest closed and locked.
Miguel tried to pick a different book.
Miguel tried to read the book, but the words jumbled on the page.
All of the above
questions.explanation: All three events happened in sequence: First, Miguel tried to choose a different book because The Secret Garden didn’t seem dangerous enough. Then the chest lid closed and locked. Finally, when he opened to page 181, the words scrambled on the page before he was transported into the story.

questions.question: Miguel regretted climbing into the loft at the beginning of the story because ___.
questions.evidence: A small wave of excitement flittered through his feathers, but his first instinct about this story soon returned. This was not going to be the exciting adventure he hoped for. He regretted having climbed into the loft.
choices: he was afraid he would be in danger again
he was afraid of heights and didn’t like flying
he didn’t think the adventure would be very exciting
all of the above
questions.explanation: After discovering the secret garden entrance as a robin, Miguel felt that 'This was not going to be the exciting adventure he hoped for.' He wanted danger and excitement, not a peaceful garden story. He regretted climbing into the loft because the adventure didn’t seem exciting enough.

questions.question: What is Miguel’s father probably thinking at the end of the story?
questions.evidence: His dad looked up. “Back from your break?” Miguel nodded. He grabbed a shovel, slipped on his gloves, and put on a cap. “This is for you,” he said, handing his dad the lavender rose. “Thanks.” “For what?”
choices: He is determined that Miguel will finish his chores.
He would rather have Miguel go help his mother and sister in the shop.
He is angry with Miguel for leaving him alone to do the work in the garden.
He is not only puzzled but also pleased that Miguel is now willing to help in the garden.
questions.explanation: Miguel’s dad is puzzled because Miguel suddenly appears with a lavender rose and thanks him for the garden—something Miguel would never have done before. But the dad is also pleased because Miguel is now willingly grabbing a shovel to help work in the garden.

questions.question: What was unusual about this adventure for Miguel?
questions.evidence: Never in any Great Gallardo book had Miguel become an animal. He pointed his beak and dove downward.
choices: He had turned into an animal in a book.
He had become a character in a book.
He was able to return to the loft whenever he chose.
He could not figure out which story he had been put into.
questions.explanation: What was unusual is that Miguel became an animal—a robin—for the first time. The text explicitly states: 'Never in any Great Gallardo book had Miguel become an animal.' In previous adventures, he had become characters like Huckleberry Finn and the Scarecrow, but never an animal.

questions.question: Why was Miguel excited when Dickon started talking to him?
questions.evidence: This must be Mary’s friend Dickon; he had a knack for talking with animals. Surely he could help Miguel.
choices: Dickon could save him from the fox that was chasing him.
Dickon could understand animals when they talked.
Miguel could help Dickon find Mary and the magical tree.
Dickon knew about the secret garden and would tell Mary.
questions.explanation: Miguel was excited because Dickon had 'a knack for talking with animals.' Since Miguel was stuck as a robin and couldn’t communicate with Mary in English, he hoped Dickon could understand his bird language and help him get his message to Mary.

questions.question: What happened to Miguel just before he awoke in a cloth-filled box?
questions.evidence: A pain shot through his body. He hit the ground with a thud. He tried to stand, but his wings felt heavy, as though he wore an iron blanket. The eyes of the fox burned into his. Miguel’s eyes fluttered. Then all went dark. Miguel awoke to warmth—a cozy feeling.
choices: He hid in Dickon’s pocket.
He took Colin to the garden.
Dickon finally understood his language.
He was knocked to the ground by the fox.
questions.explanation: Just before Miguel awoke in the cloth-filled box, the fox swatted him down during the chase. A pain shot through his body, he hit the ground, and then all went dark. He woke up in Dickon’s box filled with shredded cloth.

questions.question: Why did Miguel go to the loft?
questions.evidence: Miguel took a break in the coolness of the shop. Mom and Teresa were baking bread, so he sneaked away to the loft. He was in desperate need of The Great Gallardo’s books. Every time Miguel entered a story, he usually had to solve a big problem. Sometimes it was pretty dangerous. And danger was what Miguel needed.
choices: to get away from his mother
to find adventure and danger
to find a quiet place where he could nap
all of the above
questions.explanation: Miguel went to the loft because he wanted adventure and danger. He was 'in desperate need of The Great Gallardo’s books,' which transported him into stories where he could solve problems and face danger—exactly what he craved instead of working in the garden.

questions.question: What does indecipherable mean?
questions.evidence: Robin talk was indecipherable! Miguel stared at Dickon, willing him to understand his request. But instead, the dark eyes of the fox stared back.
choices: to be easily read
to lift high into the air
to not be able to be understood
to be dangerous and frightening
questions.explanation: The glossary defines 'indecipherable' as 'difficult or impossible to understand.' In the story, Miguel’s robin talk was indecipherable because Dickon and the fox could not understand his attempts to ask for help finding Mary.

questions.question: Why was Miguel thanking his dad at the end of the story? Use evidence from the text to support your answer.
questions.explanation: Miguel thanked his dad because the magical adventure in The Secret Garden helped him understand what his father had been trying to tell him all along—that gardens can be magic and that the Earth has healing powers. Through his experience as a robin, Miguel saw how the garden brought healing to Colin, Mary, and Dickon. When he returned home, he appreciated the garden’s beauty for the first time, giving his dad a lavender rose and grabbing a shovel to help work.
opinionPrompts: What did Miguel’s dad say about gardens at the beginning of the story?
How did the garden help the characters in The Secret Garden?
What evidence from the text shows that Miguel’s attitude changed?

grammar.title: Adverbs
grammar.explanation: Adverbs are words that describe how, when, where, or to what degree something happens. They modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. Many adverbs end in -ly.
rules: Adverbs often answer the questions How? When? Where? or To what degree?
Many adverbs are formed by adding -ly to an adjective: quick → quickly, careful → carefully.
Adverbs can modify verbs: He quickly remembered. (How did he remember? Quickly.)
Some adverbs do NOT end in -ly: gently, fast, very, well, up, down.
Adverbs can also modify adjectives: a very dangerous adventure (How dangerous? Very dangerous.)

wordBuilding.sentence: Ever so carefully, Miguel jumped down to a chair.
He quickly remembered that robins didn’t speak English.
Miguel let a current of air gently lower him to a branch of ivy.

exercises.sentence: He quickly remembered that robins didn’t speak English.
exercises.hint: Which word tells HOW Miguel remembered?

exercises.sentence: Miguel let a current of air gently lower him to a branch of ivy.
exercises.hint: Which word tells HOW the air lowered Miguel?

exercises.sentence: Ever so carefully, Miguel jumped down to a chair.
exercises.hint: Which word tells HOW Miguel jumped?

exercises.sentence: His feet floated off the ground.
exercises.hint: Which word tells WHERE his feet floated?

exercises.sentence: Never in any Great Gallardo book had Miguel become an animal.
exercises.hint: Which word tells WHEN Miguel had become an animal? (Hint: it means 'not ever.')

exercises.question: Which word is the adverb in this sentence? “Miguel quickly grabbed the shovel.”
exercises.explanation: 'Quickly' is the adverb because it tells HOW Miguel grabbed the shovel. It modifies the verb 'grabbed.'

exercises.question: Which word is the adverb in this sentence? “The feathers insulated him from the cold.”
exercises.sentence: The feathers insulated him from the cold.
options: There is no adverb in this sentence.
exercises.explanation: This sentence has no adverb! 'Feathers' is a noun (subject), 'insulated' is a verb, 'him' is a pronoun, and 'from the cold' is a prepositional phrase.

exercises.question: Which sentence contains an adverb?
options: A pain shot through his body.
exercises.explanation: 'Closer' is an adverb in 'The fox sped closer.' It tells HOW the fox sped—it moved in a closer direction.

exercises.sentence: Ever so _____ (careful), Miguel jumped down to a chair, then onto the floor.
exercises.explanation: The adjective 'careful' becomes the adverb 'carefully' by adding -ly. The adverb tells HOW Miguel jumped.

exercises.sentence: Miguel let a current of air _____ (gentle) lower him to a branch of ivy.
exercises.explanation: The adjective 'gentle' becomes the adverb 'gently' by dropping the -e and adding -ly. The adverb tells HOW the air lowered him.

exercises.sentence: He _____ (quick) remembered that robins didn’t speak English.
exercises.explanation: The adjective 'quick' becomes the adverb 'quickly' by adding -ly. The adverb tells HOW Miguel remembered.

exercises.question: Find the compound word in this sentence: “Miguel rose on an updraft and cruised above the trees.”
exercises.sentence: Miguel rose on an updraft and cruised above the trees.
exercises.explanation: 'Updraft' is a compound word made from 'up' + 'draft.' It means an air current that moves upward.

exercises.question: Find the compound word: “Miguel and Teresa often lent a hand at the Ventura family sandwich shop.”
exercises.sentence: Miguel and Teresa often lent a hand at the Ventura family sandwich shop.
exercises.explanation: 'Sandwich' is a compound word made from 'sand' + 'wich.' It was named after the Earl of Sandwich!

exercises.question: What compound word from the story means ‘without life’?
exercises.sentence: Most of the other plants seemed dry and lifeless.
exercises.explanation: 'Lifeless' is a compound word made from 'life' + 'less.' The suffix '-less' means 'without,' so 'lifeless' means 'without life.'

groaningAboutGardening.title: Desperate
groaningAboutGardening.content: Miguel was in DESPERATE need of The Great Gallardo’s books. DESPERATE means having an urgent or extreme need. Have you ever been DESPERATE to do something?

groaningAboutGardening.title: Dangerous
groaningAboutGardening.content: Miguel wanted DANGEROUS adventures—the kind that could get you hurt! DANGEROUS means likely to cause harm. But sometimes the most DANGEROUS adventures teach us the most!

groaningAboutGardening.title: Miguel’s Personality
groaningAboutGardening.content: Miguel wants ADVENTURE AND DANGER, not peace and gardening. He thinks gardens are boring. But his dad says gardens can be MAGIC. Who do you think is right?

groaningAboutGardening.title: Adverbs: How Miguel Did Things
groaningAboutGardening.content: Look for adverbs in this section! “He SNEAKED away to the loft.” How did he go? Secretly! “The Great Gallardo’s magic could be RISKY.” RISKY is an adjective describing the magic.

groaningAboutGardening.title: Sandwich
groaningAboutGardening.content: The Venturas run a SANDWICH shop! SAND + WICH = SANDWICH. This compound word is named after the Earl of Sandwich, who asked for meat between two slices of bread.

birdTalk.title: Insulated
birdTalk.content: Miguel’s feathers INSULATED him from the cold. INSULATED means PROTECTED from heat or cold. Bird feathers trap warm air close to the body—just like a winter jacket INSULATES you!

birdTalk.title: Three-Dimensional
birdTalk.content: From high above, details popped out as though they were THREE-DIMENSIONAL. THREE-DIMENSIONAL means having height, width, AND depth—like a real object, not a flat drawing!

birdTalk.title: Instinct
birdTalk.content: Miguel’s first INSTINCT told him this wouldn’t be exciting. INSTINCT means a natural feeling or reaction—something you just KNOW without thinking. Like when you pull your hand away from something hot!

birdTalk.title: Updraft
birdTalk.content: Miguel rose on an UPDRAFT! An UPDRAFT is a current of air that moves UPWARD. Birds and gliders use UPDRAFTS to rise higher without flapping! It’s UP + DRAFT = UPDRAFT.

birdTalk.title: Muster
birdTalk.content: Miguel sang the best bird-song he could MUSTER. MUSTER means to GATHER or bring forth. When you MUSTER your courage, you gather up all your bravery!

birdTalk.title: Miguel’s Communication Problem
birdTalk.content: Miguel can think in English, but his robin body only makes chirps and cheeps! He tried to say ‘Follow me, Mary!’ but it came out as ‘cheerily, cheer up.’ How will he solve this problem?

birdTalk.title: Adverbs: Quickly & Gently
birdTalk.content: “He QUICKLY remembered that robins didn’t speak English.” QUICKLY is an adverb—it tells HOW Miguel remembered. QUICK + -LY = QUICKLY!

aSnackForAFox.title: Spewed
aSnackForAFox.content: Miguel’s words SPEWED out like a bird alarm! SPEWED means POURRED OUT forcefully. Like a volcano SPEWS lava, Miguel’s frustrated words POURED OUT as tuktuk tuktuk!

aSnackForAFox.title: Indecipherable
aSnackForAFox.content: Robin talk was INDECIPHERABLE! INDECIPHERABLE means IMPOSSIBLE TO UNDERSTAND. No matter what Miguel said, Dickon couldn’t understand a single word!

aSnackForAFox.title: Swivel
aSnackForAFox.content: With a SWIVEL of his head, Miguel lifted his wings. SWIVEL means to TURN in a circle, like how a computer chair SWIVELS around. SWIV + -el = SWIVEL!

aSnackForAFox.title: The Fox Chase
aSnackForAFox.content: Miguel is in REAL danger now! The fox sees him as a TASTY TREAT. Miguel’s words came out as a bird alarm—tuktuk, tuktuk—which probably attracted the fox’s attention instead of Dickon’s help!

aSnackForAFox.title: The Fox Attack Step by Step
aSnackForAFox.content: 1) The fox’s eyes narrowed → 2) Miguel SWIVELS his head → 3) The fox LUNGES → 4) Miguel tries to fly but can’t → 5) He hides in a hollow log → 6) The fox swats him DOWN → 7) All goes dark!

aSnackForAFox.title: Updraft
aSnackForAFox.content: Remember UPDRAFT from earlier? UP + DRAFT = a current of air going UP. Compound words are two words joined together to make a NEW meaning!

brokenWing.title: Mahogany
brokenWing.content: Miguel led them down dark MAHOGANY hallways. MAHOGANY is a type of wood with a rich REDDISH-BROWN color. It’s used for fine furniture. The hallways in the big manor were made of this beautiful wood!

brokenWing.title: Celebratory
brokenWing.content: Miguel sang a CELEBRATORY song for Colin. CELEBRATORY means showing HAPPINESS about a positive event. When you sing ‘Happy Birthday,’ that’s a CELEBRATORY song!

brokenWing.title: Miguel Never Gives Up
brokenWing.content: Even with a BROKEN WING, Miguel hopped out of the box, jumped to a chair, then to the floor—all to show Mary the way to the garden. Pain flashed through his wing, but he kept going!

brokenWing.title: Dickon’s Kindness
brokenWing.content: Dickon saved Miguel from the fox and cared for his broken wing. Dickon is the kind of person who talks to animals and helps anyone in need—even a robin he just met!

brokenWing.title: Adverbs: Carefully
brokenWing.content: “Ever so CAREFULLY, Miguel jumped down to a chair.” CAREFUL + -LY = CAREFULLY. This adverb tells HOW Miguel jumped—with great care because of his broken wing!

growingMagic.title: Gardens ARE Magic!
growingMagic.content: Miguel’s dad said gardens could be magic—and he was RIGHT! The garden brought healing to Colin, joy to Mary and Dickon, and peace to Miguel. Maybe the Earth DOES have healing powers!

growingMagic.title: Miguel’s Transformation
growingMagic.content: Remember how Miguel said ‘I don’t want peace! I want adventure and danger!’? Now he’s grabbed a shovel and is happily working in the garden. His adventure taught him that gardens have their own kind of magic!

growingMagic.title: Miguel’s Complete Journey
growingMagic.content: 1) Groans about gardening → 2) Enters The Secret Garden as a robin → 3) Can’t communicate with Mary → 4) Fox breaks his wing → 5) Leads trio to the garden despite injury → 6) Returns home transformed → 7) Thanks his dad and joins the garden work!

growingMagic.title: Adverbs in the Final Scene
growingMagic.content: In the last scene, Miguel ‘waved his arms around the garden.’ AROUND is an adverb telling WHERE he waved. Miguel has come FULL CIRCLE—from reluctant gardener to enthusiastic helper!
